text.title: Cognitive Walkthrough Examples
text.summary: Two worked examples (sign-up flow and file upload) showing Q1-Q4 evaluation per step with identified issues and concrete fixes.

example.note: Step: Click "Get Started" button. Persona: first-time user, no prior experience with the product.
example.code:
Q1: Will try to achieve right effect? YES Evidence: Clear value proposition above button establishes goal.
Q2: Will notice correct action available? PARTIAL Evidence: Button is visible but competes with "Sign In" at equal visual weight.
Q3: Will associate action with effect? NO Evidence: "Get Started" is ambiguous — could mean sign up, take a demo, or browse.
Q4: Will see progress? YES Evidence: Clicking navigates to registration form — clear state change.
Issue: Q3 failure — "Get Started" label does not communicate sign-up intent. Fix: Change label to "Create Free Account" — removes ambiguity, communicates both action and cost.

example.note: Step: Drag file to upload zone. Persona: first-time user familiar with desktop file management.
example.code:
Q1: Will try to achieve right effect? PARTIAL Evidence: Users familiar with drag-drop will attempt; users who expect a browse button will look for it first and may not discover the drag zone.
Q2: Will notice correct action available? YES Evidence: Large, centered drop zone is visible and prominent.
Q3: Will associate action with effect? YES Evidence: Drop zone icon and "Drag files here" label clearly communicate the interaction.
Q4: Will see progress? NO Evidence: No upload progress indicator; no filename displayed during upload; page appears unchanged while upload runs.
Issue: Q4 failure — no feedback during file upload creates uncertainty and abandonment risk. Fix: Add progress bar showing percentage and filename. Display "Uploading..." state immediately on file drop before server confirmation.

rule.statement: Evaluate one step at a time. Feed the agent (a) the persona description, (b) a screenshot of the current screen, (c) the intended next action. Require JSON output with Q1-Q4 answers, evidence citations, and a concrete fix for every No or Partial.
rule.rationale: Multi-step context causes theme contamination — earlier step findings bias the agent's evaluation of later steps. Stateless per-step evaluation produces independent findings that can be merged without contamination.

example.note: Agent prompt template for one step evaluation.
example.code:
You are evaluating ONE step of a user task using the Cognitive Walkthrough method.
Persona: <<<one-paragraph first-time user persona>>> Goal: <<<task goal>>> Step <N> action: <<<exact intended action>>> Screen: <attached image>
Answer ONLY in JSON: {q1:{ans:"yes|no|partial", evidence:""}, q2:{ans:"yes|no|partial", evidence:""}, q3:{ans:"yes|no|partial", evidence:""}, q4:{ans:"yes|no|partial", evidence:""}, suggested_fix:""}. For each "no" or "partial", evidence MUST quote a visible UI element or explicitly state what is missing. Do not assume capabilities the persona lacks.
